narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 67.4% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize students with stronger projected outcomes. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record in undergraduate and early law school years, alongside other factors like your LSAT score, family income bracket, and plans to study full-time. The three biggest influences on this decision were your Year 3 grade decile, your Year 1 grade decile, and your undergraduate GPA. Most critically, your position in the second decile for Year 3 grades—well below the fifth or sixth decile typical for those who passed the bar—strongly pushed the model toward predicting failure, as it signals challenges in sustaining high performance under law school pressures. Similarly, landing in the second decile for Year 1 grades reinforced this concern, deviating sharply from the top-third standing common among successful bar takers and highlighting early struggles that often persist. On a brighter note, your solid 3.70 undergraduate GPA, which exceeds the 3.24 average of those who passed, worked in your favor by pulling the prediction somewhat toward success and showing your foundational strengths. While your LSAT score of 31 fell short of the 37 average for passers, and your middle-income background aligns with over a third of successful students, these played supporting roles compared to your deciles. Full-time enrollment, like nearly all who pass, was neutral or slightly positive. Compared to typical accepted applicants—who boast higher early-law deciles around 6, balancing any moderate LSAT dips with consistent performance—your profile raised red flags about bar readiness despite your impressive undergrad record. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven call to protect the program's bar passage rates. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking targeted tutoring to climb those law school deciles in future terms, or exploring part-time programs or gap-year prep courses that have helped others in similar positions strengthen their trajectories before reapplying. With focused effort on these areas, you can reshape your profile for a stronger shot next time.
